title: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems
definition: First theorem: Any consistent formal system capable of expressing basic arithmetic contains statements that are true but unprovable within the system. Second theorem: Such a system cannot prove its own consistency.

proofs.description: Gödel's original proof using Gödel numbering to construct self-referential statements
proofs.reference: Gödel, K. (1931). Über formal unentscheidbare Sätze der Principia Mathematica und verwandter Systeme.

difficulty_levels.beginner: In any sufficiently powerful mathematical system, there will be true statements that cannot be proved. The system also cannot prove its own consistency.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Gödel showed that arithmetic contains 'undecidable' statements - true but unprovable. This shattered Hilbert's dream of a complete, consistent axiomatization of all mathematics.
difficulty_levels.expert: The theorems reveal fundamental limits of formal systems. Any consistent system extending Robinson arithmetic (Q) or Peano arithmetic (PA) is incomplete. The second theorem shows consistency proofs cannot be internalized, undermining Hilbert's program. Theorems generalize via the halting problem and undecidability.

extra_fields.historical_context: Published in 1931, shattering Hilbert's program to axiomatize all of mathematics. Gödel's work influenced Turing, Church, and the development of computer science.
extra_fields.limitations: Applies only to consistent systems capable of expressing basic arithmetic. Does not apply to trivial systems, inconsistent systems, or those with non-recursive axiom sets.
